{{Psychology sidebar|all}}
{{Short description|Interdisciplinary field of study}}

'''Moral psychology''' is the study of human thought and behavior in ethical contexts.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |date=April 19, 2006 |title=Moral Psychology: Empirical Approaches |publisher=Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University |url=https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/moral-psych-emp/#IntrWhatMoraPsyc}}</ref> Historically, the term "moral psychology" was used relatively narrowly to refer to the study of [[moral development]].<ref name="Haidt&Kesebir2010">{{cite book |last1=Haidt |first1=Jonathan |last2=Kesebir |first2=Selin |editor1-last=Fiske |editor1-first=S |editor2-last=Gilbert |editor2-first=D |editor3-last=Lindzey |editor3-first=G |title=Handbook of Social Psychology |date=2010 |publisher=Wiley |location=Hoboken NJ |pages=797–832 |edition=5 |url=http://courses.washington.edu/ccab/Haidt%20&%20Kesebir%20on%20morality%20-%20HB%20Soc%20Psych%202010.pdf |chapter=Morality}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Moral Psychology|last=Lapsley|first=Daniel K.|date=1996|publisher=Westview Press|isbn=978-0-8133-3032-7|series=Developmental psychology series|location=Boulder, Colorado|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/moralpsychology0000laps}}</ref> This field of study is interdisciplinary between the application of philosophy and psychology. Moral psychology eventually came to refer more broadly to various topics at the intersection of [[ethics]], psychology, and [[philosophy of mind]].<ref>{{citation | last1=Doris | first1=John | last2=Stich | first2= Stephen | year=2008 | title=Moral Psychology: Empirical Approaches | journal=The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy | editor-first= Edward N. | editor-last=Zalta | url=http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/moral-psych-emp/ | publisher=Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | editor-last=Jackson | editor-first=Frank | editor-last2=Smith | editor-first2=Michael | last=Wallace | first=R. Jay | title=The Oxford Handbook of Contemporary Philosophy | chapter=Moral Psychology | date=November 29, 2007 | pages=86–113 | chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H4sIErz7KUcC&pg=PA86 | publisher=OUP Oxford | isbn=978-0-19-923476-9 | quote=Moral psychology is the study of morality in its psychological dimensions}}</ref><ref name="Ellemers2019">{{cite journal |last1=Ellemers |first1=Naomi |last2=van der Toorn |first2=Jojanneke |last3=Paunov |first3=Yavor |last4=van Leeuwen |first4=Thed |title=The Psychology of Morality: A Review and Analysis of Empirical Studies Published From 1940 Through 2017 |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Review |volume=23 |issue=4 |date=18 January 2019 |pages=332–366 |doi=10.1177/1088868318811759 |pmid=30658545 |pmc=6791030 |language=en |issn=1088-8683}}</ref> Some of the main topics of the field are moral judgment, [[moral reasoning]], moral satisficing, moral sensitivity, [[moral responsibility]], moral motivation, moral identity, moral action, moral development, moral diversity, [[moral character]] (especially as related to [[virtue ethics]]), [[altruism]], [[psychological egoism]], [[moral luck]], moral forecasting, moral emotion, [[affective forecasting]], and moral disagreement.{{sfn|Doris|Stich|2008|loc=§1}}<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Teper|first1=R.|last2=Inzlicht|first2=M.|last3=Page-Gould|first3=E.|year=2011|title=Are we more moral than we think?: Exploring the role of affect in moral behavior and moral forecasting|journal=Psychological Science|volume=22|issue=4|pages=553–558|doi=10.1177/0956797611402513|pmid=21415242|citeseerx=10.1.1.1033.5192|s2cid=206585532}}</ref>

Today, moral psychology is a thriving area of research spanning many disciplines,<ref>Doris & Stich (2008), §1.</ref> with major bodies of research on the biological,<ref name="Sevinc2014">{{cite journal |last1=Sevinc |first1=Gunes |last2=Spreng |first2=R. Nathan |last3=Soriano-Mas |first3=Carles |title=Contextual and Perceptual Brain Processes Underlying Moral Cognition: A Quantitative Meta-Analysis of Moral Reasoning and Moral Emotions |journal=PLOS ONE |date=4 February 2014 |volume=9 |issue=2 |article-number=e87427 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0087427|pmid=24503959 |pmc=3913597 |bibcode=2014PLoSO...987427S |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Moll2005">{{cite journal |last1=Moll |first1=Jorge |last2=Zahn |first2=Roland |last3=de Oliveira-Souza |first3=Ricardo |last4=Krueger |first4=Frank |last5=Grafman |first5=Jordan |title=The neural basis of human moral cognition |journal=Nature Reviews Neuroscience |date=October 2005 |volume=6 |issue=10 |pages=799–809 |doi=10.1038/nrn1768|pmid=16276356 |s2cid=2915834 }}</ref> cognitive/computational<ref name="KleimanWeiner2017">{{cite journal |last1=Kleiman-Weiner |first1=Max |last2=Saxe |first2=Rebecca |last3=Tenenbaum |first3=Joshua B. |title=Learning a commonsense moral theory |journal=Cognition |date=October 2017 |volume=167 |pages=107–123 |doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2017.03.005 |pmid=28351662 |hdl=1721.1/118457 |s2cid=3184506 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Cushman2013">{{cite journal |last1=Cushman |first1=Fiery |title=Action, Outcome, and Value |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Review |date=16 July 2013 |volume=17 |issue=3 |pages=273–292 |doi=10.1177/1088868313495594 |pmid=23861355 |s2cid=18501147 }}</ref><ref name="Crockett2013">{{cite journal |last1=Crockett |first1=Molly J. |title=Models of morality |journal=Trends in Cognitive Sciences |date=August 2013 |volume=17 |issue=8 |pages=363–366 |doi=10.1016/j.tics.2013.06.005 |pmid=23845564 |pmc=3925799 }}</ref> and cultural<ref name="Henrich2005">{{cite journal |last1=Henrich |first1=Joseph |last2=Boyd |first2=Robert |last3=Bowles |first3=Samuel |last4=Camerer |first4=Colin |last5=Fehr |first5=Ernst |last6=Gintis |first6=Herbert |last7=McElreath |first7=Richard |last8=Alvard |first8=Michael |last9=Barr |first9=Abigail |last10=Ensminger |first10=Jean |last11=Henrich |first11=Natalie Smith |last12=Hill |first12=Kim |last13=Gil-White |first13=Francisco |last14=Gurven |first14=Michael |last15=Marlowe |first15=Frank W. |last16=Patton |first16=John Q. |last17=Tracer |first17=David |title="Economic man" in cross-cultural perspective: Behavioral experiments in 15 small-scale societies |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |date=22 December 2005 |volume=28 |issue=6 |pages=795–815 |doi=10.1017/S0140525X05000142 |pmid=16372952 |s2cid=3194574 |url=https://authors.library.caltech.edu/2278/1/HENbbs05.pdf }}</ref><ref name="Purzycki2016">{{cite journal |last1=Purzycki |first1=Benjamin Grant |last2=Apicella |first2=Coren |last3=Atkinson |first3=Quentin D. |last4=Cohen |first4=Emma |last5=McNamara |first5=Rita Anne |last6=Willard |first6=Aiyana K. |last7=Xygalatas |first7=Dimitris |last8=Norenzayan |first8=Ara |last9=Henrich |first9=Joseph |title=Moralistic gods, supernatural punishment and the expansion of human sociality |journal=Nature |date=10 February 2016 |volume=530 |issue=7590 |pages=327–330 |doi=10.1038/nature16980|pmid=26863190 |url=https://pure.au.dk/ws/files/96835878/nature16980.pdf |bibcode=2016Natur.530..327P |s2cid=205247725 }}</ref> basis of moral judgment and behavior, and a growing body of research on moral judgment in the context of artificial intelligence.<ref name="Awad2018">{{cite journal |last1=Awad |first1=Edmond |last2=Dsouza |first2=Sohan |last3=Kim |first3=Richard |last4=Schulz |first4=Jonathan |last5=Henrich |first5=Joseph |last6=Shariff |first6=Azim |last7=Bonnefon |first7=Jean-François |last8=Rahwan |first8=Iyad |title=The Moral Machine experiment |journal=Nature |date=24 October 2018 |volume=563 |issue=7729 |pages=59–64 |doi=10.1038/s41586-018-0637-6 |pmid=30356211 |bibcode=2018Natur.563...59A |hdl=10871/39187 |s2cid=53029241 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Bonnefon2016">{{cite journal |last1=Bonnefon |first1=J.-F. |last2=Shariff |first2=A. |last3=Rahwan |first3=I. |title=The social dilemma of autonomous vehicles |journal=Science |date=23 June 2016 |volume=352 |issue=6293 |pages=1573–1576 |doi=10.1126/science.aaf2654 |pmid=27339987 |bibcode=2016Sci...352.1573B |arxiv=1510.03346 |s2cid=35400794 }}</ref>

==History==
The origins of moral psychology can be traced back to early philosophical works, largely concerned with moral education, such as by [[Plato]] and [[Aristotle]] in Ancient Greece,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Carr |first1=David |title=Metaphysics and methods in moral enquiry and education: Some old philosophical wine for new theoretical bottles |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=26 August 2014 |volume=43 |issue=4 |pages=500–515 |doi=10.1080/03057240.2014.943167|s2cid=145588696 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lewis |first1=Paul |title=In defence of Aristotle on character: toward a synthesis of recent psychology, neuroscience and the thought of Michael Polanyi |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=June 2012 |volume=41 |issue=2 |pages=155–170 |doi=10.1080/03057240.2012.668005|s2cid=146755766 }}</ref> as well as from the [[Buddhist ethics|Buddhist]]<ref>Goodman, Charles, "Ethics in Indian and Tibetan Buddhism", The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Summer 2021 Edition), Edward N. Zalta (ed.), URL = <https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2021/entries/ethics-indian-buddhism/>.</ref> and [[Confucianism#Social morality and ethics|Confucian]] traditions.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fengyan |first1=Wang |title=Confucian thinking in traditional moral education: key ideas and fundamental features |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=December 2004 |volume=33 |issue=4 |pages=429–447 |doi=10.1080/0305724042000327984|s2cid=216114943 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Murray |first1=Judson B. |title=Educating human nature: 'nature' and 'nurture' in early Confucian moral education |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=December 2012 |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=509–527 |doi=10.1080/03057240.2012.721759|s2cid=144739301 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wei |first1=Tan Tai |title=Some Confucian Insights and Moral Education |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=January 1990 |volume=19 |issue=1 |pages=33–37 |doi=10.1080/0305724900190104}}</ref> Empirical studies of moral judgment go back at least as far as the 1890s with the work of [[Frank Chapman Sharp]],<ref name="Sharp1898">{{cite journal |last1=Sharp |first1=Frank Chapman |title=An Objective Study of Some Moral Judgments |journal=The American Journal of Psychology |date=January 1898 |volume=9 |issue=2 |pages=198–234 |doi=10.2307/1411759 |jstor=1411759 }}</ref> coinciding with the development of psychology as a discipline separate from philosophy. Since at least 1894, philosophers and psychologists attempted to empirically evaluate the morality of an individual,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=May |first1=Mark A. |last2=Hartshorne |first2=Hugh |title=Objective Methods of Measuring Character |journal=The Pedagogical Seminary and Journal of Genetic Psychology |date=March 1925 |volume=32 |issue=1 |pages=45–67 |doi=10.1080/08856559.1925.10532317}}</ref><ref name="Pittel1966">{{cite journal |last1=Pittel |first1=Stephen M. |last2=Mendelsohn |first2=Gerald A. |title=Measurement of moral values: A review and critique. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=1966 |volume=66 |issue=1 |pages=22–35 |doi=10.1037/h0023425|pmid=5329602 }}</ref> especially attempting to distinguish adults from children in terms of their judgment. Unfortunately, these efforts failed because they "attempted to quantify how much morality an individual had—a notably contentious idea—rather than understand the individual's psychological representation of morality".<ref name="Wendorf2001">{{cite journal|last=Wendorf|first=Craig A|year=2001|title=History of American morality research, 1894–1932|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/247411256|journal=History of Psychology|volume=4|issue=3|pages=272–288|doi=10.1037/1093-4510.4.3.272}}</ref>{{rp|page=284}}

{{Quote box|align=right|width=40%|quoted=1|quote=[I]f you said that you studied moral psychology in the 1980s, then you probably studied the development of moral reasoning. You didn't need to agree with Kohlberg on any particular claim, but you lived and worked on land that Kohlberg had cleared.|source=Jonathan Haidt<ref name="Haidt2013">{{cite journal |last1=Haidt |first1=Jonathan |title=Moral psychology for the twenty-first century |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=September 2013 |volume=42 |issue=3 |pages=281–297 |doi=10.1080/03057240.2013.817327|s2cid=144638008 }}</ref>{{rp|page=282}}}}

In most introductory psychology courses, students learn about moral psychology by studying the psychologist [[Lawrence Kohlberg]],<ref>{{cite thesis |last=Kohlberg |first=Lawrence |title=The development of modes of moral thinking and choice in the years 10 to 16. |date=1958 |degree=PhD |location=Chicago |oclc=1165315}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{cite book |title=The Measurement of Moral Judgment |last1=Colby |first1=Anne |last2=Kohlberg |first2=Lawrence |date=1987 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-32565-3 |series=Standard Issue Scoring Manual |volume=2 |location=Cambridge }}</ref><ref>{{cite book|title=Handbook of Socialization Theory and Research|chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofsocial0000gosl|chapter-url-access=registration|last=Kohlberg|first=L.|date=1969|publisher=Rand McNally|editor-last=Goslin|editor-first=David|location=Chicago|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofsocial0000gosl/page/347 347–480]|chapter=Stage and sequence: The cognitive development approach to socialization}}</ref> who proposed a highly influential [[Kohlberg's stages of moral development|theory of moral development]], developed throughout the 1950s and 1960s. This theory was built on [[Jean Piaget|Piaget]]'s observation that children develop intuitions about justice that they can later articulate.{{Citation needed|date=September 2024}} Kohlberg proposed [[Lawrence Kohlberg's stages of moral development|six stages]] broken into three categories of moral reasoning that he believed to be universal to all people in all cultures.<ref>{{Citation|last=Kohlberg|first=Lawrence|editor3-first=Edmund|editor3-last=Sullivan|editor2-first=Brian S|editor2-last=Crittenden|editor1-first=Clive M|editor1-last=Beck|chapter=1. Stages of moral development as a basis for moral education|date=1971-01-31|publisher=University of Toronto Press|isbn=978-1-4426-5675-8|doi=10.3138/9781442656758-004|title=Moral Education|pages=23–92}}</ref> The increasing sophistication of justice-based reasoning was taken as a sign of development. Moral cognitive development, in turn, was assumed to be a necessary (but not sufficient) condition for moral action.<ref name="Kohlberg1977">{{cite journal |last1=Kohlberg |first1=Lawrence |last2=Hersh |first2=Richard H. |title=Moral development: A review of the theory |journal=Theory into Practice |date=1977 |volume=16 |issue=2 |pages=53–59 |doi=10.1080/00405847709542675}}</ref>

But researchers using the Kohlberg model found a gap between what people said was most moral and actions they took. In response, Augusto Blasi proposed his self-model<ref>{{Cite book |last=Lapsley |first=Daniel |title=Developing the Virtues |date=2016-09-22 |chapter=Moral Self-Identity and the Social-Cognitive Theory of Virtue |pages=34–68 |chapter-url=https://academic.oup.com/book/7321/chapter/152068808 |language=en |doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780190271466.003.0003|isbn=978-0-19-027146-6 }}</ref> that links ideas of moral judgment and action through moral commitment. Those with moral goals central to the self-concept are more likely to take moral action, as they feel a greater obligation to do so. Those who are motivated will attain a unique moral identity.<ref name="Hardy, S. A. 2011">{{cite journal|last1=Hardy|first1=S. A.|last2=Carlo|first2=G.|year=2011|title=Moral identity: What is it, how does it develop, and is it linked to moral action?|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/229948988|journal=Child Development Perspectives|volume=5|issue=3|pages=212–218|doi=10.1111/j.1750-8606.2011.00189.x}}</ref>

Following the independent publication of a pair of landmark papers in 2001 (respectively led by Jonathan Haidt and Joshua Greene),<ref name="Haidt2001">{{cite journal|last=Haidt|first=Jonathan|date=October 2001|title=The Emotional Dog and Its Rational Tail|url=http://fitelson.org/confirmation/reasoning_adler_rips.pdf#page=1036|journal=Psychological Review|volume=108|issue=4|pages=814–834|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.108.4.814|pmid=11699120|citeseerx=10.1.1.620.5536}}</ref><ref name="Greene2001">{{cite journal |last1=Greene |first1=J. D. |last2=Sommerville |first2=R Brian |last3=Nystrom |first3=Leigh E |last4=Darley |first4=John M. |last5=Cohen |first5=Jonathan D |title=An fMRI Investigation of Emotional Engagement in Moral Judgment |journal=Science |date=14 September 2001 |volume=293 |issue=5537 |pages=2105–2108 |doi=10.1126/science.1062872 |pmid=11557895 |bibcode=2001Sci...293.2105G|s2cid=1437941 }}</ref> there was a surge in interest in moral psychology across a broad range of subfields of psychology, with interest shifting away from developmental processes towards a greater emphasis on social, cognitive, affective and neural processes involved in moral judgment.<ref name="Haidt&Kesebir2010" /><ref name="Ellemers2019" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cohen Priva |first1=Uriel |last2=Austerweil |first2=Joseph L. |title=Analyzing the history of Cognition using Topic Models |journal=Cognition |date=February 2015 |volume=135 |pages=4–9 |doi=10.1016/j.cognition.2014.11.006|pmid=25497481 |s2cid=37146919 }}</ref>

==Methods==
[[File:Trolley problem.png|thumb|The trolley problem, a commonly-used moral dilemma in psychological research]]

Philosophers, psychologists and researchers from other fields have created various methods for studying topics in moral psychology, with empirical studies dating back to at least the 1890s.<ref name="Wendorf2001" /> The methods used in these studies include moral dilemmas such as the [[trolley problem]],<ref name="Sharp1898" /><ref name="Greene2001" /> structured interviews and surveys as a means to study moral psychology and its development, as well as the use of economic games,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Story |first1=Giles W. |last2=Vlaev |first2=Ivo |last3=Metcalfe |first3=Robert D. |last4=Crockett |first4=Molly J. |last5=Kurth-Nelson |first5=Zeb |last6=Darzi |first6=Ara |last7=Dolan |first7=Raymond J. |title=Social redistribution of pain and money |journal=Scientific Reports |date=30 October 2015 |volume=5 |issue=1 |article-number=15389 |doi=10.1038/srep15389|pmid=26515529 |pmc=4626774 |bibcode=2015NatSR...515389S }}</ref> neuroimaging,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Moll |first1=Jorge |last2=de Oliveira-Souza |first2=Ricardo |last3=Eslinger |first3=Paul J. |last4=Bramati |first4=Ivanei E. |last5=Mourão-Miranda |first5=Janaı́na |last6=Andreiuolo |first6=Pedro Angelo |last7=Pessoa |first7=Luiz |title=The Neural Correlates of Moral Sensitivity: A Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging Investigation of Basic and Moral Emotions |journal=The Journal of Neuroscience |date=1 April 2002 |volume=22 |issue=7 |pages=2730–2736 |doi=10.1523/JNEUROSCI.22-07-02730.2002 |pmid=11923438 |pmc=6758288}}</ref> and studies of natural language use.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sagi |first1=Eyal |last2=Dehghani |first2=Morteza |title=Measuring Moral Rhetoric in Text |journal=Social Science Computer Review |date=31 October 2013 |volume=32 |issue=2 |pages=132–144 |doi=10.1177/0894439313506837|s2cid=62259852 }}</ref>

===Interview techniques===
In 1963, Lawrence Kohlberg presented an approach to studying differences in moral judgment by modeling evaluative diversity as reflecting a series of developmental stages (à la Jean Piaget). [[Lawrence Kohlberg's stages of moral development]] are:<ref name="higheststage">{{cite journal | last=Kohlberg | first=Lawrence | title=The Claim to Moral Adequacy of a Highest Stage of Moral Judgment | journal=Journal of Philosophy | volume=70 | year=1973 | pages=630–646 | doi=10.2307/2025030 | issue=18 | jstor=2025030 }}</ref>
# Obedience and punishment orientation
# Self-interest orientation
# Interpersonal accord and conformity
# Authority and social-order maintaining orientation
# Social contract orientation
# Universal ethical principles

Stages 1 and 2 are combined into a single stage labeled "pre-conventional", and stages 5 and 6 are combined into a single stage labeled "post-conventional" for the same reason; psychologists can consistently categorize subjects into the resulting four stages using the "Moral Judgement Interview" which asks subjects why they endorse the answers they do to a standard set of moral dilemmas.<ref name=":2" />

===Survey instruments===
Between 1910 and 1930, in the United States and Europe, several morality tests were developed to classify subjects as either fit or unfit to make moral judgments.<ref name=Wendorf2001/><ref name="Verplaetse2008">{{cite journal | last=Verplaetse | first=Jan | title=Measuring the moral sense: morality tests in continental Europe between 1910 and 1930 | journal= Paedagogica Historica | volume=44 | issue=3 | year=2008 | pages=265–286 | doi=10.1080/00309230701722721| s2cid=143771452 }}</ref> Test-takers would classify or rank standardized lists of personality traits, hypothetical actions, or pictures of hypothetical scenes. As early as 1926, catalogs of personality tests included sections specifically for morality tests, though critics persuasively argued that they merely measured intelligence or awareness of social expectations.<ref name="Wendorf2001" />

Meanwhile, Kohlberg inspired a new series of morality tests. The [[Defining Issues Test]] (dubbed "Neo-Kohlbergian" by its constituents) scores relative preference for post-conventional justifications,<ref>{{cite book|title=Development in Judging Moral Issues|last=Rest|first=James R.|date=1979|publisher=University of Minnesota Press|isbn=978-0-8166-0891-1|location=Minneapolis}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rest |first1=James |last2=Narvaez |first2=Darcia |last3=Bebeau |first3=Muriel |last4=Thoma |first4=Stephen |title=A Neo-Kohlbergian Approach: The DIT and Schema Theory |journal=Educational Psychology Review |date=1999 |volume=11 |issue=4 |pages=291–324 |doi=10.1023/A:1022053215271|s2cid=14483253 }}</ref> and the Moral Judgment Test scores consistency of one's preferred justifications.<ref name="Lind1978">{{cite book|title=Sozialisation und Moral|last=Lind|first=Georg|publisher=Beltz|year=1978|isbn=978-3-407-51134-8|editor-last=Portele|editor-first=G.|location=Weinheim|pages=171–201|language=de|trans-title=Socialization and Morality|chapter=Wie misst man moralisches Urteil? Probleme und alternative Möglichkeiten der Messung eines komplexen Konstrukts|trans-chapter=How do you measure moral judgment? Problems and alternative ways of measuring a complex construct|oclc=715635639}}</ref><ref name="Lind2008">{{cite book |last1=Lind |first1=Georg |editor1-last=Fasko |editor1-first=Daniel Jr |editor2-last=Willis |editor2-first=Wayne |title=Contemporary Philosophical and Psychological Perspectives on Moral Development and Education |date=2008 |publisher=Hampton Press |pages=185–220 |chapter=The meaning and measurement of moral judgment competence: A dual-aspect model}}</ref> Both treat evaluative ability as similar to [[IQ]] (hence the single score), allowing categorization by high score vs. low score.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

Among the more recently developed survey measures, the Moral Foundations Questionnaire<ref name="Graham2011">{{cite journal |last1=Graham |first1=Jesse |last2=Nosek |first2=Brian A. |last3=Haidt |first3=Jonathan |last4=Iyer |first4=Ravi |last5=Koleva |first5=Spassena |last6=Ditto |first6=Peter H. |title=Mapping the moral domain. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2011 |volume=101 |issue=2 |pages=366–385 |doi=10.1037/a0021847|pmc=3116962 |pmid=21244182 |bibcode=2011JPSP..101..366G }}</ref> is a widely used survey measure of the five moral intuitions proposed by [[Moral Foundations Theory]]: care/harm, fairness/cheating, loyalty/betrayal, authority/subversion, and sanctity/degradation. The questions ask respondents to rate various considerations in terms of how relevant they are to the respondent's moral judgments. The purpose of the questionnaire is to measure the degree to which people rely upon each of the five moral intuitions (which may coexist). The new and improved version of this instrument (i.e., Moral Foundations Questionnaire-2; MFQ-2) was developed in 2023. In this version, Fairness was split to Equality and Proportionality. Hence, the MFQ-2 measures Care, Equality, Proportionality, Loyalty, Authority, and Purity.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Atari |first1=Mohammad |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |last3=Graham |first3=Jesse |last4=Koleva |first4=Sena |last5=Stevens |first5=Sean T. |last6=Dehghani |first6=Morteza |date=November 2023 |title=Morality beyond the WEIRD: How the nomological network of morality varies across cultures. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=125 |issue=5 |pages=1157–1188 |doi=10.1037/pspp0000470 |pmid=37589704 |issn=1939-1315|doi-access=free }}</ref> In addition to survey instruments measuring endorsement of moral foundations, a number of other contemporary survey measures exist relating to other broad taxonomies of moral values,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Curry |first1=Oliver Scott |last2=Jones Chesters |first2=Matthew |last3=Van Lissa |first3=Caspar J. |title=Mapping morality with a compass: Testing the theory of 'morality-as-cooperation' with a new questionnaire |journal=Journal of Research in Personality |date=February 2019 |volume=78 |pages=106–124 |doi=10.1016/j.jrp.2018.10.008|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Janoff-Bulman |first1=Ronnie |last2=Carnes |first2=Nate C. |title=Social Justice and Social Order: Binding Moralities across the Political Spectrum |journal=PLOS ONE |date=31 March 2016 |volume=11 |issue=3 |article-number=e0152479 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0152479|pmid=27031103 |pmc=4816418 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1152479J |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Guerra |first1=Valeschka M. |last2=Giner-Sorolla |first2=Roger |title=The Community, Autonomy, and Divinity Scale (CADS): A New Tool for the Cross-Cultural Study of Morality |journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology |date=January 2010 |volume=41 |issue=1 |pages=35–50 |doi=10.1177/0022022109348919|s2cid=145410595 |url=https://kar.kent.ac.uk/36650/1/Guerra%20%26%20Giner-Sorolla%20-%20CADS%20manuscript.pdf }}</ref> as well as more specific moral beliefs,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bastian |first1=Brock |last2=Bain |first2=Paul |last3=Buhrmester |first3=Michael D. |last4=Gómez |first4=Ángel |last5=Vázquez |first5=Alexandra |last6=Knight |first6=Clinton G. |last7=Swann |first7=William B. |title=Moral Vitalism: Seeing Good and Evil as Real, Agentic Forces |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=August 2015 |volume=41 |issue=8 |pages=1069–1081 |doi=10.1177/0146167215589819|pmid=26089349 |s2cid=11280774 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ståhl |first1=Tomas |last2=Zaal |first2=Maarten P. |last3=Skitka |first3=Linda J. |title=Moralized Rationality: Relying on Logic and Evidence in the Formation and Evaluation of Belief Can Be Seen as a Moral Issue |journal=PLOS ONE |date=16 November 2016 |volume=11 |issue=11 |article-number=e0166332 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0166332|pmid=27851777 |pmc=5112873 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1166332S |doi-access=free }}</ref> or concerns.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ho |first1=Arnold K. |last2=Sidanius |first2=Jim |last3=Kteily |first3=Nour |last4=Sheehy-Skeffington |first4=Jennifer |last5=Pratto |first5=Felicia |last6=Henkel |first6=Kristin E. |last7=Foels |first7=Rob |last8=Stewart |first8=Andrew L. |title=The nature of social dominance orientation: Theorizing and measuring preferences for intergroup inequality using the new SDO<sub>7</sub> scale. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=December 2015 |volume=109 |issue=6 |pages=1003–1028 |doi=10.1037/pspi0000033|pmid=26479362 |bibcode=2015JPSP..109.1003H |url=https://researchonline.lse.ac.uk/id/eprint/88104/ }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Duckitt |first1=John |last2=Bizumic |first2=Boris |title=Multidimensionality of Right-Wing Authoritarian Attitudes: Authoritarianism-Conservatism-Traditionalism: Authoritarianism-Conservatism-Traditionalism |journal=Political Psychology |date=December 2013 |volume=34 |issue=6 |pages=841–862 |doi=10.1111/pops.12022}}</ref>

==Evolutionary origins==
[[File:IMG-20180218-WA0095.jpg|thumb|Cooperative behavior has been observed in many nonhuman animals.]]
{{Main|Evolution of morality}}
{{See also|Evolutionary ethics|Pragmatic ethics#Moral ecology}}

According to Haidt,<ref name="Haidt2013" /> the belief that morality is not innate was one of the few theoretical commitments uniting many of the prominent psychologists studying morality in the twentieth century (with some exceptions<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Petrinovich |first1=Lewis |last2=O'Neill |first2=Patricia |last3=Jorgensen |first3=Matthew |title=An empirical study of moral intuitions: Toward an evolutionary ethics. |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=1993 |volume=64 |issue=3 |pages=467–478 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.64.3.467}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Krebs |first1=Dennis L. |last2=Denton |first2=Kathy |last3=Wark |first3=Gillian |title=The Forms and Functions of Real-life Moral Decision-making |journal=Journal of Moral Education |date=June 1997 |volume=26 |issue=2 |pages=131–145 |doi=10.1080/0305724970260202}}</ref>). A substantial amount of research in recent decades has focused on the [[evolution]]ary origins of various aspects of morality.<ref>{{cite book |editor1-last=Sinnott-Armstrong |editor1-first=Walter |title=Moral Psychology, Volume 1: The Evolution of Morality: Adaptations and Innateness |date=2007 |volume=1 |publisher=A Bradford Book |isbn=978-0-262-69354-7 |url=https://mitpress.mit.edu/books/moral-psychology-volume-1}}</ref><ref name="Brosnan2014">{{cite journal |last1=Brosnan |first1=S. F. |last2=de Waal |first2=F. B. M. |title=Evolution of responses to (un)fairness |journal=Science |date=18 September 2014 |volume=346 |issue=6207 |article-number=1251776 |doi=10.1126/science.1251776 |pmid=25324394 |pmc=4451566 }}</ref><ref name="Tomasello2011">{{cite journal |last1=Tomasello |first1=Michael |last2=Vaish |first2=Amrisha |title=Origins of Human Cooperation and Morality |journal=[[Annual Review of Psychology]] |date=3 January 2013 |volume=64 |issue=1 |pages=231–255 |doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-113011-143812|pmid=22804772 |hdl=10161/13649 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hare2017">{{cite journal |last1=Hare |first1=Brian |title=Survival of the Friendliest: Evolved via Selection for Prosociality |journal=[[Annual Review of Psychology]] |date=3 January 2017 |volume=68 |issue=1 |pages=155–186 |doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-010416-044201 |pmid=27732802 |s2cid=3387266 }}</ref>

In ''Unto Others: the Evolution and Psychology of Unselfish Behavior'' (1998), [[Elliott Sober]] and [[David Sloan Wilson]] demonstrated that diverse moralities could evolve through [[group selection]].<ref name=SoberWilson1998/> In particular, they dismantled the idea that [[natural selection]] will favor a homogeneous population in which all creatures care only about their own personal welfare and/or behave only in ways which advance their own personal reproduction.<ref name=SoberWilson1998>{{cite book|title=Unto Others: The Evolution and Psychology of Unselfish Behavior|last1=Sober|first1=Elliott|last2=Wilson|first2=David Sloan|publisher=Harvard University Press|year=1998|isbn=978-0-674-93046-9|oclc=37761960|location=Cambridge|url-access=registration|url=https://archive.org/details/untoothersevolut00sobe}}</ref>

Tim Dean has advanced the more general claim that moral diversity would evolve through [[frequency-dependent selection]] because each moral approach is vulnerable to a different set of situations which threatened our ancestors.<ref name=Dean2012>{{cite journal|title= Evolution and moral diversity |author=Dean, Tim |journal=Baltic International Yearbook of Cognition, Logic and Communication|year=2012|volume=7|doi=10.4148/biyclc.v7i0.1775|doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Topics and theories==

===Moral identity===
{{Main|Moral identity}}
Moral identity refers to the importance of morality to a person's identity, typically construed as either a trait-like individual difference, or set of chronically accessible schemas.<ref name="Hardy, S. A. 2011" /><ref name="Hardy&Carlo2011" >{{cite book |last1=Hardy |first1=Sam A. |last2=Carlo |first2=Gustavo |editor1-last=Schwartz |editor1-first=Seth J. |editor2-last=Luyckx |editor2-first=Koen |editor3-last=Vignoles |editor3-first=Vivian L. |title=Handbook of identity theory and research |date=2011 |publisher=Springer |isbn=978-1-4419-7988-9 |pages=495–513 |chapter=Moral Identity}}</ref> There are considered to be two main levels of perspective on moral identity. One of them the trait-based perspective theory where certain personality traits are triggered during moral situations. The second perspective is the socio-cognitive perspective where these moral identities are a "self-schema" that will occur due to the social environment.<ref name="Krettenauer 330–345">{{Cite journal |last=Krettenauer |first=Tobias |date=2020-07-02 |title=Moral identity as a goal of moral action: A Self-Determination Theory perspective |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/03057240.2019.1698414 |journal=Journal of Moral Education |language=en |volume=49 |issue=3 |pages=330–345 |doi=10.1080/03057240.2019.1698414 |issn=0305-7240|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Moral identity is theorized to be one of the key motivational forces connecting moral reasoning to moral behavior,<ref name="Hardy&Carlo2011" /> as suggested by a 2016 meta-analysis reporting that moral identity is positively (albeit only modestly) associated with moral behavior.<ref name="Hertz&Krettenauer2016">{{cite journal |last1=Hertz |first1=Steven G. |last2=Krettenauer |first2=Tobias |title=Does Moral Identity Effectively Predict Moral Behavior?: A Meta-Analysis |journal=Review of General Psychology |date=1 June 2016 |volume=20 |issue=2 |pages=129–140 |doi=10.1037/gpr0000062|s2cid=148276515 |url=https://scholars.wlu.ca/psyc_faculty/112 |doi-access=free |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Although moral identity mainly focuses on a moral action there is sometimes "moral disengagement" that will take place to reduce the negative consequences of an action or lack of action.<ref name="Krettenauer 330–345"/>

=== Moral satisficing ===
The theory of moral satisficing applies the study of ecological rationality to moral behavior.<ref>{{Citation |title=Moral Intuition = Fast and Frugal Heuristics? |date=2007 |work=Moral Psychology |publisher=The MIT Press |doi=10.7551/mitpress/7573.003.0003 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0024-FB30-8 |isbn=978-0-262-30301-9|hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gigerenzer |first=Gerd |date=2010-05-12 |title=Moral Satisficing: Rethinking Moral Behavior as Bounded Rationality |journal=Topics in Cognitive Science |volume=2 |issue=3 |pages=528–554 |doi=10.1111/j.1756-8765.2010.01094.x |pmid=25163875 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0024-F5F4-4 |issn=1756-8757|hdl-access=free }}</ref> In this view, much of moral behavior is based on social heuristics rather than traits, virtues, or utilitarian calculations. Social heuristics are a form of satisficing, a term coined by Nobel laureate Herbert Simon.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Simon |first=H. A. |date=1956 |title=Rational choice and the structure of the environment. |journal=Psychological Review |volume=63 |issue=2 |pages=129–138 |doi=10.1037/h0042769 |pmid=13310708 |issn=1939-1471}}</ref> Social heuristics are not good or bad, or beneficial or harmful, per se, but solely in relation to the environments in which they are used. For instance, an adolescent may commit a crime not because of an evil character or a utilitarian calculation but due to following the social heuristic "do what your peers do." After shifting to a different peer group, the same person's behavior may shift to a more socially desirable outcome – by relying on the very same heuristic. From this perspective, moral behavior is thus not simply a consequence of inner virtue or traits, but a function of both the mind and the environment, a view based on Simon's scissors analogy.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Simon |first=H. |date=1990-01-01 |title=Invariants Of Human Behavior |journal=Annual Review of Psychology |volume=41 |issue=1 |pages=1–19 |doi=10.1146/annurev.psych.41.1.1 |pmid=18331187 |issn=0066-4308}}</ref> Many other moral theories, in contrast, consider the mind alone, such as Kohlberg's state theory, identity theories, virtue theories, and willpower theories.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

The ecological perspective has methodological implications for the study of morality: According to it, behavior needs to be studied in social groups and not only in individuals, in natural environments and not only in labs. Both principles are violated, for instance, by the study of how individuals respond to artificial trolley problems. The theory of moral satisficing also has implications for moral policy, implying that problematic behavior can be changed by changing the environment, not only the individual.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

Darwin argued that one original function of morality was the coherence and coordination of groups.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Darwin |first=Charles |title=The descent of man, and selection in relation to sex |date=1871 |publisher=J. Murray |location=London|doi=10.5962/bhl.title.2092 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/18365 }}</ref> This suggests that social heuristics that generate coherence and coordination are also those that guide moral behavior. These social heuristics include imitate-your-peers, equality (divide a resource equally), and tit-for-tat (be kind first, then imitate your partner's behavior).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gigerenzer |first1=Gerd |last2=Gaissmaier |first2=Wolfgang |date=2011-01-10 |title=Heuristic Decision Making |journal=Annual Review of Psychology |volume=62 |issue=1 |pages=451–482 |doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-120709-145346 |pmid=21126183 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0024-F16D-5 |issn=0066-4308|hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |title=Simple Heuristics in a Social World |date=2012-11-29 |publisher=Oxford University Press |doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780195388435.001.0001 |isbn=978-0-19-538843-5 |editor-last=Hertwig |editor-first=Ralph |editor-last2=Hoffrage |editor-first2=Ulrich |editor-last3=Research Group |editor-first3=ABC}}</ref> In general, the social heuristics of individuals or institutions shape their moral fabric.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

Moral satisficing explains two phenomena that pose a puzzle for virtue and trait theories: moral luck and systematic inconsistencies, as when teens who voluntarily made a virginity pledge were just as likely to have premarital sex as their peers who did not.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rosenbaum |first=Janet Elise |date=2009-01-01 |title=Patient Teenagers? A Comparison of the Sexual Behavior of Virginity Pledgers and Matched Nonpledgers |journal=Pediatrics |volume=123 |issue=1 |pages=e110–e120 |doi=10.1542/peds.2008-0407 |pmid=19117832 |pmc=2768056 |hdl=20.500.12648/8444 |issn=0031-4005}}</ref> From an ecological view of morality, such inconsistencies are to be expected when individuals move from one environment to another.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

Nagel (1979, p.&nbsp;59) defines moral luck as follows: ''Where a significant aspect of what someone does depends on factors beyond his control, yet we continue to treat him in that respect as an object of moral judgment, it can be called moral luck.''<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Nagel |first=T. |date=1979 |title=Moral luck. Mortal Questions |journal=[New York: Cambridge University Press, 1979] |pages=31–32}}</ref> Others voiced concerns that moral luck poses a limit to improving our moral behavior and makes it difficult to evaluate behavior as right or wrong.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Matheson |first1=David S |last2=Kissoon |first2=Niranjan |date=2006 |title=A comparison of decision-making by physicians and administrators in healthcare settings |journal=Critical Care |volume=10 |issue=5 |page=163 |doi=10.1186/cc5028 |doi-access=free |pmid=16959045 |pmc=1751052 |issn=1364-8535}}</ref> Yet this concern is based on an internal view of the causes of moral behavior; from an ecological view, moral luck is an inevitable consequence of the interaction between mind and environment. A teen is morally lucky to have not grown up in a criminal peer group, and an adult is morally lucky to have not been conscripted into an army.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

Moral satisficing postulates that behavior is guided by social heuristics, not by moral rules such as "don't kill", as assumed in theories of moral heuristics<ref>{{Cite journal |last=sunstein |first=cass r. |date=August 2005 |title=moral heuristics |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=28 |issue=4 |pages=531–542 |doi=10.1017/s0140525x05000099 |pmid=16209802 |issn=0140-525X}}</ref> or in Hauser's "moral grammar" with hard-wired moral rules.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hauser |first=Marc D. |date=2006-12-01 |title=The liver and the moral organ |journal=Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience |volume=1 |issue=3 |pages=214–220 |doi=10.1093/scan/nsl026 |pmid=18985108 |pmc=2555424 |issn=1749-5024}}</ref> Moral satisficing postulates that moral rules are essentially social heuristics that ultimately serve the coordination and cooperation of social groups.{{cn|date=April 2026}}

===Moral values===
{{See also|Value (ethics and social sciences)|Theory of Basic Human Values}}

Psychologist [[Shalom H. Schwartz|Shalom Schwartz]] defines individual values as "conceptions of the desirable that guide the way social actors (e.g. organisational leaders, policymakers, individual persons) select actions, evaluate people at events, and explain their actions and evaluations."<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Schwartz|first1=S. H.|year=1999|title=A Theory of Cultural Values and Some Implications for Work |url=http://130.18.86.27/faculty/warkentin/SecurityPapers/Merrill/Schwartz1999_APIR48_1_CulturalValues.pdf |journal=Applied Psychology: An International Review |volume=48 |issue=1 |pages=23–47 |doi=10.1080/026999499377655}}</ref> Cultural values form the basis for social norms, laws, customs and practices. While individual values vary case by case (a result of unique life experience), the average of these values point to widely held cultural beliefs (a result of shared cultural values).

Kristiansen and Hotte<ref name="Kristiansen&Hotte1996">{{cite conference|last1=Kristiansen|first1=Connie M|last2=Hotte|first2=Alan M|date=1996|title=Morality and the self: Implications for the when and how of value-attitude-behavior relations|conference=The Psychology of Values: The Ontario Symposium on Personality and Social Psychology|publisher=Erlbaum Hillsdale, NJ|volume=8|pages=77–105}}</ref> reviewed many research articles regarding people's values and attitudes and whether they guide behavior. With the research they reviewed and their own extension of Ajzen and Fishbein's [[theory of reasoned action]], they conclude that value-attitude-behavior depends on the individual and their moral reasoning. Another issue that Kristiansen and Hotte discovered through their research was that individuals tended to "create" values to justify their reactions to certain situations, which they called the "value justification hypothesis".<ref name="Kristiansen&Hotte1996" /> Their theory is comparable to Jonathan Haidt's [[Social intuitionism|social intuitionist theory]],<ref name="Haidt2001" /> where individuals justify their intuitive emotions and actions through post-hoc moral reasoning.

Kristiansen and Hotte also found that independent selves had actions and behaviors that are influenced by their own thoughts and feelings, but [[Interdependent]] selves have actions, behaviors and self-concepts that were based on the thoughts and feelings of others. Westerners have two dimensions of emotions, activation and pleasantness. The Japanese have one more, the range of their interdependent relationships. Markus and Kitayama found that these two different types of values had different motives. Westerners, in their explanations, show self-bettering biases. Easterners, on the other hand, tend to focus on "other-oriented" biases.<ref name="Kristiansen&Hotte1996" />

====Moral foundations theory====
{{Main|Moral foundations theory}}
Moral foundations theory, first proposed in 2004 by Jonathan Haidt and Craig Joseph,<ref name="Haidt2004">{{cite journal|last=Haidt|first=Jonathan|author2=Craig Joseph|title=Intuitive ethics: how innately prepared intuitions generate culturally variable virtues|journal=Daedalus|date=Fall 2004|volume=133|issue=4|pages=55–66|url=http://faculty.virginia.edu/haidtlab/articles/haidt.joseph.2004.intuitive-ethics.pub035.pdf|doi=10.1162/0011526042365555|s2cid=1574243|access-date=2012-10-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160909124829/http://faculty.virginia.edu/haidtlab/articles/haidt.joseph.2004.intuitive-ethics.pub035.pdf|archive-date=2016-09-09}}</ref> attempts to explain the origins of and variation in human moral reasoning on the basis of innate, modular foundations.<ref name="Graham2013">{{cite book | last1=Graham | first1=J. | last2=Haidt | first2=J. | last3=Koleva | first3=S. | last4=Motyl | first4=M. | last5=Iyer | first5=R. | last6=Wojcik | first6=S. | last7=Ditto | first7=P.H. | title=Moral Foundations Theory: The pragmatic validity of moral pluralism | journal=Advances in Experimental Social Psychology | volume=47 | year=2013 | pages=55–130 | url=http://www-bcf.usc.edu/~jessegra/papers/GHKMIWD.inpress.MFT.AESP.pdf | doi=10.1016/b978-0-12-407236-7.00002-4 | isbn=978-0-12-407236-7 | s2cid=2570757 | access-date=2015-01-11 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170731065520/http://www-bcf.usc.edu/~jessegra/papers/GHKMIWD.inpress.MFT.AESP.pdf | archive-date=2017-07-31 }}</ref> Notably, moral foundations theory has been used to describe the difference between the moral foundations of political liberals and political conservatives.<ref name="Haidt&Graham2007">{{cite journal |last1=Haidt |first1=Jonathan |last2=Graham |first2=Jesse |title=When Morality Opposes Justice: Conservatives Have Moral Intuitions that Liberals may not Recognize |journal=Social Justice Research |date=23 May 2007 |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=98–116 |doi=10.1007/s11211-007-0034-z|s2cid=6824095 }}</ref><ref name="Graham2009">{{cite journal|last1=Graham|first1=Jesse|last2=Haidt|first2=Jonathan|last3=Nosek|first3=Brian A.|year=2009|title=Liberals and conservatives rely on different sets of moral foundations|url=http://projectimplicit.net/nosek/papers/GHN2009.pdf|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=96|issue=5|pages=1029–1046|doi=10.1037/a0015141|pmid=19379034}}</ref> Haidt and Joseph expanded on previous research done by Shweder and his three ethics theory.<ref name="Haidt2004" /> Shweder's theory consisted of three moral ethics: the ethics of community, autonomy, and divinity.<ref name="Shweder1997">{{cite book |last1=Shweder |first1=Richard |last2=Much |first2=Nancy |last3=Mahapatra |first3=Manamohan |last4=Park |first4=Lawrence |editor1-last=Brandt |editor1-first=Allan |editor2-last=Rozin |editor2-first=Paul |title=Morality and Health |publisher=Routledge |pages=119–169 |date=1997 |chapter=The "big three" of morality (autonomy, community, divinity) and the "big three" explanations of suffering.}}</ref> Haidt and Joseph's original formulation proposed four candidate foundations (suffering, hierarchy, reciprocity, and purity).<ref name="Haidt2004"></ref> Haidt and Graham took this theory and extended it to discuss the five psychological systems that more specifically make up the three moral ethics theory. These Five Foundations of Morality and their importance vary throughout each culture and construct virtues based on their emphasized foundation. 
The five psychological foundations are:
* Harm/care, which starts with the sensitivity to signs of suffering in offspring and develops into a general dislike of seeing suffering in others and the potential to feel compassion in response.
* Fairness/reciprocity, which is developed when someone observes or engages in reciprocal interactions. This foundation is concerned with virtues related to fairness and justice. 
* Ingroup/loyalty, which constitutes recognizing, trusting, and cooperating with members of one's ingroup as well as being wary of members of other groups.
* Authority/respect, which is how someone navigates in a hierarchal ingroups and communities.
* Purity/sanctity, which stems from the emotion of disgust that guards the body by responding to elicitors that are biologically or culturally linked to disease transmission.
The five foundations theory is both a [[Psychological nativism|nativist]] and cultural-psychological theory. Modern moral psychology concedes that "morality is about protecting individuals" and focuses primarily on issues of justice (harm/care and fairness/reciprocity).<ref name="Haidt&Graham2007" />{{rp|page=99}} Their research found that "justice and related virtues...make up half of the moral world for liberals, while justice-related concerns make up only one fifth of the moral world for conservatives".<ref name="Haidt&Graham2007" />{{rp|page=99}} Liberals value harm/care and fairness/reciprocity significantly more than the other moralities, while conservatives value all five equally. Ownership has also been argued to be a strong candidate to be a moral foundation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Atari |first1=Mohammad |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |date=2023-10-10 |title=Ownership is (likely to be) a moral foundation |journal=The Behavioral and Brain Sciences |volume=46 |article-number=e326 |doi=10.1017/S0140525X2300119X |issn=1469-1825 |pmid=37813408}}</ref>

===Moral virtues===
In 2004, D. Lapsley and D. Narvaez outlined how [[social cognition]] explains aspects of moral functioning.<ref>{{cite book|title=Moral Development, Self, and Identity|last1=Lapsley|first1=Daniel K.|last2=Narvaez|first2=Darcia|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2004|isbn=978-1-135-63233-5|pages=189–212|chapter=A social-cognitive approach to the moral personality|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=q054AgAAQBAJ&pg=PA189}}</ref> Their social cognitive approach to personality has six critical resources of moral personality: cognition, self-processes, affective elements of personality, changing social context, lawful situational variability, and the integration of other literature. Lapsley and Narvaez suggest that moral values and actions stem from more than our virtues and are controlled by a set of self-created [[Schema (psychology)|schemas]] (cognitive structures that organize related concepts and integrate past events). They claim that schemas are "fundamental to our very ability to notice dilemmas as we appraise the moral landscape" and that over time, people develop greater "moral expertise".{{Sfn|Lapsley|Narvaez|2004|p=197}}

==== Triune ethics theory ====
{{Main|Triune ethics Meta-theory}}
The triune ethics meta-theory (TEM) has been proposed by Darcia Narvaez as a [[metatheory]] that highlights the relative contributions to moral development of [[biological inheritance]] (including human evolutionary [[adaptations]]), environmental influences on neurobiology, and the role of culture.<ref name=":6">{{cite journal|last=Narvaez|first=Darcia|date=March 1, 2008|title=Triune ethics: The neurobiological roots of our multiple moralities|url=https://www3.nd.edu/~dnarvaez/TriuneEthicsWord.htm|journal=New Ideas in Psychology|volume=26|issue=1|pages=95–119|doi=10.1016/j.newideapsych.2007.07.008|issn=0732-118X|citeseerx=10.1.1.152.4926}}</ref> TET proposes three basic mindsets that shape ethical behavior: self-protectionism (a variety of types), engagement, and imagination (a variety of types that are fueled by protectionism or engagement). A mindset influences perception, affordances, and rhetorical preferences. Actions taken within a mindset become an ethic when they trump other values. Engagement and communal imagination represent optimal human functioning that are shaped by the evolved developmental niche (evolved nest) that supports optimal psychosocial neurobiological development.<ref name="Narváez, Darcia 2009">{{cite book |title=Neurobiology and the Development of Human Morality: Evolution, Culture and Wisdom |last1=Narvaez |first1=Darcia  |publisher=WWNorton|year=2014|isbn=978-0-393-70655-0}}</ref>  Based on worldwide anthropological research (e.g., Hewlett and Lamb's Hunter-Gatherer Childhoods), Narvaez uses small-band hunter-gatherers as a baseline for the evolved nest and its effects.

===Moral reasoning and development===
{{main|Moral reasoning|Moral development}}
{{see also|Moral education|Values education}}
Moral development and reasoning are two overlapping topics of study in moral psychology that have historically received a great amount of attention, even preceding the influential work of Piaget and Kohlberg.<ref name="Wendorf2001" /> Moral reasoning refers specifically to the study of how people think about right and wrong and how they acquire and apply moral rules.<ref name="Pizarro2007a">{{cite book |last1=Pizarro |first1=David A. |editor1-last=Baumeister |editor1-first=Roy F |editor2-last=Vohs |editor2-first=Kathleen F |title=Encyclopedia of Social Psychology |url=https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasoci00baum |url-access=limited |date=2007 |publisher=SAGE Publications, Inc. |isbn=978-1-4129-5625-3 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasoci00baum/page/n632 591]–592 |chapter=Moral Reasoning|doi=10.4135/9781412956253.n352 }}</ref> Moral development refers more broadly to age-related changes in thoughts and emotions that guide moral beliefs, judgments and behaviors.<ref name="Barnett2007">{{cite book |last1=Barnett |first1=Mark A. |editor1-last=Baumeister |editor1-first=Roy F |editor2-last=Vohs |editor2-first=Kathleen D |title=Encyclopedia of Social Psychology |url=https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasoci00baum |url-access=limited |date=2007 |publisher=SAGE Publications, Inc. |isbn=978-1-4129-5625-3 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasoci00baum/page/n628 587] |chapter=Moral Development|doi=10.4135/9781412956253.n349 }}</ref>

====Kohlberg's stage theory====
{{main|Lawrence Kohlberg's stages of moral development}}
Jean Piaget, in watching children play games, noted how their rationales for cooperation changed with experience and maturation.<ref name="Piaget1948">{{cite book |last1=Piaget |first1=Jean |title=The Moral Judgment of the Child |date=1948 |publisher=Free Press |url=https://www.burmalibrary.org/docs20/Piaget-moral_judgment_of_the_child-en-ocr-tu.pdf}}</ref> He identified two stages, [[wikt:heteronomous|heteronomous]] (morality centered outside the self) and autonomous (internalized morality). [[Lawrence Kohlberg|Lawrence Kohlberg]] sought to expand Piaget's work. His cognitive developmental theory of moral reasoning dominated the field for decades. He focused on moral development as one's progression in the capacity to reason about justice. Kohlberg's interview method included hypothetical moral dilemmas or conflicts of interest (most notably, the [[Heinz dilemma]]). He proposed six stages and three levels of development (claiming that "anyone who interviewed children about dilemmas and who followed them longitudinally in time would come to our six stages and no others).<ref>{{cite book|title=The Psychology of Moral Development: The Nature and Validity of Moral Stages|last=Kohlberg|first=Lawrence|publisher=Harper & Row|year=1984|isbn=978-0-06-064761-2|series=Essays on Moral Development|volume=2|page=195}}</ref> At the Preconventional level, the first two stages included the punishment-and-obedience orientation and the instrumental-relativist orientation. The next level, the conventional level, included the interpersonal concordance or "good boy – nice girl" orientation, along with the "law and order" orientation. Lastly, the final Postconventional level consisted of the social-contract, legalistic orientation and the universal-ethical-principle orientation.<ref>{{cite web|last=Crain|first=W.C.|title=Kohlberg's Stages of Moral Development |url=http://faculty.plts.edu/gpence/html/kohlberg.htm |work=Theories of Development |publisher=Prentice-Hall |access-date=October 3, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111004152005/http://faculty.plts.edu/gpence/html/kohlberg.htm |archive-date=October 4, 2011}}</ref> According to Kohlberg, an individual is considered more cognitively mature depending on their stage of moral reasoning, which grows as they advance in education and world experience.

Critics of Kohlberg's approach (such as [[Carol Gilligan]] and Jane Attanucci) argue that there is an over-emphasis on [[justice]] and an under-emphasis on an additional perspective to moral reasoning, known as the [[Ethics of care|care perspective]]. The justice perspective draws attention to inequality and oppression, while striving for reciprocal rights and equal respect for all. The care perspective draws attention to the ideas of detachment and abandonment, while striving for attention and response to people who need it. Care Orientation is relationally based. It has a more situational focus that is dependent on the needs of others as opposed to Justice Orientation's objectivity.<ref name="Attanucci 1988">{{cite book|title=Moral Development: Caring Voices and Women's Moral Frames|last1=Gilligan|first1=Carol|last2=Attanucci|first2=Jane|publisher=Taylor & Francis|year=1994|isbn=978-0-8153-1553-7|editor-last=Puka|editor-first=Bill|volume=34|pages=123–237|chapter=Two Moral Orientations: Gender Differences and Similarities|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_OBjzzmUS2oC&pg=PA123|issue=3}}</ref> However, reviews by others have found that Gilligan's theory was not supported by empirical studies since orientations are individual dependent.<ref>{{cite book|title=Handbook of Moral Development|last1=Walker|first1=Lawrence J.|last2=Smetana|first2=Judith|publisher=Psychology Press|year=2005|isbn=978-1-135-61917-6|editor-last=Killen|editor-first=Melanie|pages=93–115|chapter=Gender and Morality|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4CV5AgAAQBAJ&pg=PA93}}</ref><ref>Jaffee and Hyde (2001){{Full citation needed|date=February 2018}}</ref> In fact, in neo-Kohlbergian studies with the Defining Issues Test, females tend to get slightly higher scores than males.<ref>{{cite book|title=Postconventional Moral Thinking: A Neo-Kohlbergian Approach|last1=Rest|first1=James R.|last2=Narvaez|first2=Darcia|last3=Thoma|first3=Stephen J.|last4=Bebeau|first4=Muriel J.|publisher=Psychology Press|year=1999|isbn=978-1-135-70561-9}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=February 2018}}

==== The attachment approach to moral judgment ====
Aner Govrin's attachment approach to moral judgment<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Govrin | first1 = A | year = 2014 | title = The ABC of moral development: an attachment approach to moral judgment | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 5 | issue = 6| pages = 1–14 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2014.00006 | pmid = 24478739 | pmc = 3901400 | doi-access = free }} [[File:CC-BY icon.svg|50px]] This article contains quotations from this source, which is available under the [https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/  Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported (CC BY 3.0)] license.</ref><ref>Govrin, A. (2019). Ethics and attachment - How we make moral judgments. London: Routledge</ref><ref>Govrin, A. (2014) "From Ethics of Care to Psychology of Care: Reconnecting Ethics of Care to Contemporary Moral Psychology", Frontiers in Psychology pp. 1-10</ref> proposes that, through early interactions with the caregiver, the child acquires an internal representation of a system of rules that determine how right/wrong judgments are to be construed, used, and understood. By breaking moral situations down into their defining features, the attachment model of moral judgment outlines a framework for a universal moral faculty based on a universal, innate, deep structure that appears uniformly in the structure of almost all moral judgments regardless of their content.

===Moral behaviour===
{{See also|Social preferences}}

Historically, major topics of study in the domain of moral behavior have included [[violence]] and [[altruism]],<ref name="Staub2003">{{cite book |last1=Staub |first1=Ervin |title=Psychology of good and evil: why children, adults, and groups help and harm others. |date=2003 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-511-07031-0}}</ref><ref name="Baumeister1997">{{cite book |last1=Baumeister |first1=Roy F. |title=Evil: inside human cruelty and violence |date=1997 |publisher=W.H. Freeman |location=New York |isbn=978-0-7167-3567-0}}</ref> [[Bystander effect|bystander intervention]] and obedience to [[authority]] (e.g., the [[Milgram experiment]]<ref name="Milgram1963">{{cite journal|last=Milgram |first=Stanley |year=1963 |title=Behavioral Study of Obedience |journal=Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology |volume=67 |issue=4 |pages=371–8 |pmid=14049516 |url=http://content.apa.org/journals/abn/67/4/371 |doi=10.1037/h0040525 |citeseerx=10.1.1.599.92 }}</ref> and [[Stanford prison experiment]]<ref name="Haney1972">{{cite journal |last1=Haney |first1=Craig |last2=Banks |first2=Curtis |last3=Zimbardo |first3=Philip |title=Interpersonal Dynamics in a Simulated Prison |date=1972 |url=https://apps.dtic.mil/dtic/tr/fulltext/u2/751041.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200326122943/https://apps.dtic.mil/dtic/tr/fulltext/u2/751041.pdf|archive-date=March 26, 2020}}</ref>).<ref name="Haidt&Kesebir2010" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Martin |first1=Jack |title=Ernest Becker and Stanley Milgram: Twentieth-century students of evil. |journal=History of Psychology |date=2016 |volume=19 |issue=1 |pages=3–21 |doi=10.1037/hop0000016|pmid=26640976 }}</ref> Recent research on moral behavior uses a wide range of methods, including using [[Experience sampling method|experience sampling]] to try and estimate the actual prevalence of various kinds of moral behavior in everyday life.<ref name="Hofmann2014">{{cite journal |last1=Hofmann |first1=W. |last2=Wisneski |first2=D. C. |last3=Brandt |first3=M. J. |last4=Skitka |first4=L. J. |title=Morality in everyday life |journal=Science |date=11 September 2014 |volume=345 |issue=6202 |pages=1340–1343 |doi=10.1126/science.1251560|pmid=25214626 |bibcode=2014Sci...345.1340H |s2cid=31731176 }}</ref><ref name="Hofmann2018">{{cite journal |last1=Hofmann |first1=Wilhelm |last2=Brandt |first2=Mark J. |last3=Wisneski |first3=Daniel C. |last4=Rockenbach |first4=Bettina |last5=Skitka |first5=Linda J. |title=Moral Punishment in Everyday Life |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=30 May 2018 |volume=44 |issue=12 |pages=1697–1711 |doi=10.1177/0146167218775075|pmid=29848212 |s2cid=44154039 |url=https://pure.uvt.nl/ws/files/25376159/Moral_Punishment_in_Everyday_Life.pdf }}</ref> Research has also focused on variation in moral behavior over time, through studies of phenomena such as [[Self-licensing|moral licensing]].<ref name=monin>{{cite journal | last1 = Monin | first1 = B | last2 = Miller | first2 = D. T. | year = 2001 | title = Moral credentials and the expression of prejudice | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 81 | issue = 1 | pages = 33–43 | pmid = 11474723 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.81.1.33 | bibcode = 2001JPSP...81...33M | url = http://www.ask-force.org/web/Discourse/Monin-Moral-Credentials-Prejudice-2001.pdf | url-status = bot: unknown | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20150430050738/http://www.ask-force.org/web/Discourse/Monin-Moral-Credentials-Prejudice-2001.pdf | archive-date = 2015-04-30 }}</ref><ref name="Blanken2015">{{cite journal |last1=Blanken |first1=Irene |last2=van de Ven |first2=Niels |last3=Zeelenberg |first3=Marcel |title=A Meta-Analytic Review of Moral Licensing |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=25 February 2015 |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=540–558 |doi=10.1177/0146167215572134|pmid=25716992 |hdl=10411/20458 |s2cid=65216 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Yet other studies focusing on social preferences examine various kinds of resource allocation decisions,<ref name="Henrich2005" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Peysakhovich |first1=Alexander |last2=Nowak |first2=Martin A. |last3=Rand |first3=David G. |title=Humans display a 'cooperative phenotype' that is domain general and temporally stable |journal=Nature Communications |date=16 September 2014 |volume=5 |issue=1 |page=4939 |doi=10.1038/ncomms5939|pmid=25225950 |bibcode=2014NatCo...5.4939P |doi-access=free }}</ref> or use incentivized behavioral experiments to investigate the way people weighted their own interests against other people's when deciding whether to harm others, for example, by examining how willing people are to administer electric shocks to themselves vs. others in exchange for money.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Crockett |first1=Molly J. |last2=Kurth-Nelson |first2=Zeb |last3=Siegel |first3=Jenifer Z. |last4=Dayan |first4=Peter |last5=Dolan |first5=Raymond J. |title=Harm to others outweighs harm to self in moral decision making |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=2 December 2014 |volume=111 |issue=48 |pages=17320–17325 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1408988111|pmid=25404350 |pmc=4260587 |bibcode=2014PNAS..11117320C |doi-access=free }}</ref>

James Rest reviewed the literature on moral functioning and identified at least four components necessary for a moral behavior to take place:<ref>{{cite journal|last=Rest|first=James R|date=1983|title=Morality|journal=Handbook of Child Psychology|volume=3|pages=556–629}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Narváez|first1=Darcia|last2=Rest|first2=James|date=1995|title=The four components of acting morally|url=https://www3.nd.edu/~dnarvaez/documents/NARVAEZREST.pdf|journal=Moral Behavior and Moral Development: An Introduction|pages=385–400}}</ref>
* Sensitivity – noticing and interpreting the situation
* Reasoning and making a judgment regarding the best (most moral) option
* Motivation (in the moment but also habitually, such as moral identity)
* Implementation - having the skills and perseverance to carry out the action
Reynolds and Ceranic researched the effects of social consensus on one's moral behavior. Depending on the level of social consensus (high vs. low), moral behaviors will require greater or lesser degrees of moral identity to motivate an individual to make a choice and endorse a behavior. Also, depending on social consensus, particular behaviors may require different levels of moral reasoning.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Reynolds|first1=Scott J.|last2=Ceranic|first2=Tara L.|date=2007|title=The effects of moral judgment and moral identity on moral behavior: An empirical examination of the moral individual.|url=http://www.pauldavidtuff.com/PDF%20Files/Effects_of_Moral_Judgement.pdf|journal=Journal of Applied Psychology|volume=92|issue=6|pages=1610–1624|doi=10.1037/0021-9010.92.6.1610|pmid=18020800|issn=1939-1854}}</ref>

More recent attempts to develop an integrated model of moral motivation<ref>{{cite journal|last=Leffel|first=G. M.|date=2008|title=Who cares? Generativity and the moral emotions, part 2: A "social intuitionist model" of moral motivation|journal=Journal of Psychology and Theology|volume=36|issue=3|pages=182–201|doi=10.1177/009164710803600303|s2cid=149360947}}</ref> have identified at least six different levels of moral functioning, each of which has been shown to predict some type of moral or pro-social behavior: moral intuitions, moral emotions, moral virtues/vices (behavioral capacities), moral values, moral reasoning, and moral willpower. This [[Social intuitionism|social intuitionist]] model of moral motivation<ref>{{harvnb|Leffel|2008}}'s model draws heavily on {{harvnb|Haidt|2001}}'s social intuitionist model of moral judgment.</ref> suggests that moral behaviors are typically the product of multiple levels of moral functioning, and are usually energized by the "hotter" levels of intuition, emotion, and behavioral virtue/vice. The "cooler" levels of values, reasoning, and willpower, while still important, are proposed to be secondary to the more affect-intensive processes.

Moral behavior is also studied under the umbrella of [[personality psychology]]. Topics within personality psychology include the traits or individual differences underlying moral behavior, such as [[generativity]], self-control, [[agreeableness]], [[cooperativeness]] and [[Honesty-humility factor of the HEXACO model of personality|honesty/humility]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Baumeister |first1=Roy F. |last2=Juola Exline |first2=Julie |title=Virtue, Personality, and Social Relations: Self-Control as the Moral Muscle |journal=Journal of Personality |date=December 1999 |volume=67 |issue=6 |pages=1165–1194 |doi=10.1111/1467-6494.00086|pmid=10637991 }}</ref><ref name="Thielmann2020">{{cite journal |last1=Thielmann |first1=Isabel |last2=Spadaro |first2=Giuliana |last3=Balliet |first3=Daniel |title=Personality and prosocial behavior: A theoretical framework and meta-analysis. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=January 2020 |volume=146 |issue=1 |pages=30–90 |doi=10.1037/bul0000217|pmid=31841013 |s2cid=209384267 |url=https://zenodo.org/record/3731374 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=McAdams |first1=Dan P. |last2=Mayukha |first2=Ananya |date=2023-01-28 |title=Hiding in plain view: An historical perspective on the study of morality in personality psychology |journal=Journal of Personality |volume=92 |issue=3 |language=en |pages=666–682 |doi=10.1111/jopy.12808 |pmid=36648361 |issn=0022-3506|doi-access=free }}</ref> as well as moral change goals,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Jessie |last2=Wilt |first2=Joshua |last3=Meindl |first3=Peter |last4=Watkins |first4=Hanne M. |last5=Goodwin |first5=Geoffrey P. |date=2023-01-18 |title=How and Why People Want to Be More Moral |journal=Journal of Personality |volume=92 |issue=3 |language=en |pages=907–925 |doi=10.1111/jopy.12812 |pmid=36652292 |issn=0022-3506|doi-access=free }}</ref> among many other topics.

Regarding interventions aimed at shaping moral behavior, a 2009 meta analysis of [[business ethics]] instruction programs found that such programs have only "a minimal impact on increasing outcomes related to ethical perceptions, behavior, or awareness."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Waples |first1=Ethan P. |last2=Antes |first2=Alison L. |last3=Murphy |first3=Stephen T. |last4=Connelly |first4=Shane |last5=Mumford |first5=Michael D. |title=A Meta-Analytic Investigation of Business Ethics Instruction |journal=Journal of Business Ethics |date=June 2009 |volume=87 |issue=1 |pages=133–151 |doi=10.1007/s10551-008-9875-0|s2cid=153414285 }}</ref> A 2005 meta analysis<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lyubomirsky |first1=Sonja |last2=King |first2=Laura |last3=Diener |first3=Ed |title=The Benefits of Frequent Positive Affect: Does Happiness Lead to Success? |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=2005 |volume=131 |issue=6 |pages=803–855 |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.131.6.803|pmid=16351326 |bibcode=2005PsycB.131..803L |s2cid=684129 |url=https://www.escholarship.org/uc/item/1k08m32k }}</ref> suggested that positive affect can at least momentarily increase prosocial behavior (with subsequent meta analyses also showing that prosocial behavior reciprocally increases positive affect in the actor<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Curry |first1=Oliver Scott |last2=Rowland |first2=Lee A. |last3=Van Lissa |first3=Caspar J. |last4=Zlotowitz |first4=Sally |last5=McAlaney |first5=John |last6=Whitehouse |first6=Harvey |title=Happy to help? A systematic review and meta-analysis of the effects of performing acts of kindness on the well-being of the actor |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=May 2018 |volume=76 |pages=320–329 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2018.02.014|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hui |first1=Bryant P. H. |last2=Ng |first2=Jacky C. K. |last3=Berzaghi |first3=Erica |last4=Cunningham-Amos |first4=Lauren A. |last5=Kogan |first5=Aleksandr |title=Rewards of kindness? A meta-analysis of the link between prosociality and well-being. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |date=3 September 2020 |volume=146 |issue=12 |pages=1084–1116 |doi=10.1037/bul0000298|pmid=32881540 |s2cid=221497259 }}</ref>).

====Value-behavior consistency====
{{See also|Value-action gap|Moral disengagement}}
In looking at the relations between moral values, attitudes, and behaviors, previous research asserts that there is less correspondence between these three aspects than one might assume.<ref name="Darnell2019">{{cite journal |last1=Darnell |first1=Catherine |last2=Gulliford |first2=Liz |last3=Kristjánsson |first3=Kristján |last4=Paris |first4=Panos |title=Phronesis and the Knowledge-Action Gap in Moral Psychology and Moral Education: A New Synthesis? |journal=Human Development |date=2019 |volume=62 |issue=3 |pages=101–129 |doi=10.1159/000496136|s2cid=150535431 |url=http://nectar.northampton.ac.uk/11799/3/Darnell_etal_KARGER_2019_Phronesis_and_the_Knowledge_Action_Gap_in_Moral_Psychology_and_Moral_Education_A_New_Synthesis.pdf }}</ref> In fact, it seems to be more common for people to label their behaviors with a justifying value rather than having a value beforehand and then acting on it. There are some people that are more likely to act on their personal values: those low in self-monitoring and high in self-consciousness, due to the fact that they are more aware of themselves and less aware of how others may perceive them. Self-consciousness here means being literally more conscious of yourself, not fearing judgement or feeling anxiety from others. Social situations and the different categories of norms can be telling of when people may act in accordance with their values, but this still is not concrete either. People will typically act in accordance with social, contextual and personal norms, and there is a likelihood that these norms can also follow one's moral values. Though there are certain assumptions and situations that would suggest a major value-attitude-behavior relation, there is not enough research to confirm this phenomenon.

====Moral willpower====
{{Main|Ego depletion|Self-control}}
{{See also|Delayed gratification|Social cognitive theory of morality}}
Building on earlier work by Metcalfe and [[Walter Mischel#Self-control|Mischel]] on delayed gratification,<ref name=":3">{{cite journal|last1=Metcalfe|first1=J.|last2=Mischel|first2=W.|year=1999|title=A hot/cool-system analysis of delay of gratification: Dynamics of willpower|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/13101564|journal=Psychological Review|volume=106|issue=1|pages=3–19|doi=10.1037/0033-295x.106.1.3|pmid=10197361}}</ref> Baumeister, Miller, and Delaney explored the notion of willpower by first defining the self as being made up of three parts: reflexive consciousness, or the person's awareness of their environment and of himself as an individual; interpersonal being, which seeks to mold the self into one that will be accepted by others; and executive function.<ref name=":4">{{cite book|title=Judeo-Christian Perspectives on Psychology: Human Nature, Motivation, and Change|last=Baumeister|publisher=American Psychological Association|year=2005|isbn=978-1-59147-161-5|editor-last=Miller|editor-first=William|location=Washington, DC|pages=57–72|chapter=Self and volition|editor-last2=Delaney|editor-first2=Harold}}</ref> They stated, "[T]he self can free its actions from being determined by particular influences, especially those of which it is aware".{{Sfn|Baumeister|2005|p=68}} The three prevalent theories of willpower describe it as a limited supply of energy, as a cognitive process, and as a skill that is developed over time. Research has largely supported that willpower works like a "moral muscle" with a limited supply of strength that may be depleted (a process referred to as Ego depletion), conserved, or replenished, and that a single act requiring much self-control can significantly deplete the "supply" of willpower.<ref name=":4" /> While exertion reduces the ability to engage in further acts of willpower in the short term, such exertions actually improve a person's ability to exert willpower for extended periods in the long run.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal|last1=Muraven|first1=Mark|last2=Baumeister|first2=Roy F.|last3=Tice|first3=Dianne M.|date=August 1, 1999|title=Longitudinal Improvement of Self-Regulation Through Practice: Building Self-Control Strength Through Repeated Exercise|url=https://www.albany.edu/~muraven/publications/promotion%20files/articles/muraven,%20baumeister,%20&%20tice,%201999.pdf|journal=The Journal of Social Psychology|volume=139|issue=4|pages=446–457|doi=10.1080/00224549909598404|issn=0022-4545|pmid=10457761}}</ref> Additional research has been conducted that may [[Ego depletion#Reproducibility controversy and conflicting meta analyses|cast doubt on the idea of ego-depletion]].<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.dropbox.com/s/c17hie7wh4yaohd/Hagger%20et%20al%20%282016%29%20A%20Multilab%20Preregistered%20Replication%20of%20the%20Ego-Depletion%20Effect.pdf?dl=0 | title=Hagger et al (2016) A Multilab Preregistered Replication of the Ego-Depletion Effect.pdf}}</ref>

===Moral intuitions===
{{Main|Social intuitionism|Dual process theory (moral psychology)}}
In 2001, Jonathan Haidt introduced his [[Social intuitionism|social intuitionist model]] which claimed that with few exceptions, moral judgments are made based upon socially derived intuitions. Moral intuitions happen immediately, automatically, and [[Unconscious mind|unconsciously]], with reasoning largely serving to generate post-hoc rationalizations to justify one's instinctual reactions.<ref name="Haidt2001" /> He provides four arguments to doubt causal importance of reason. Firstly, Haidt argues that since there is a [[Dual process theory|dual process system]] in the brain when making automatic evaluations or assessments, this same process must be applicable to moral judgement as well. The second argument, based on research on [[motivated reasoning]], claims that people behave like "intuitive lawyers", searching primarily for evidence that will serve motives for social relatedness and attitudinal coherence. Thirdly, Haidt found that people have post hoc reasoning when faced with a moral situation, this ''a posteriori'' (after the fact) explanation gives the illusion of objective moral judgement but in reality is subjective to one's gut feeling. Lastly, research has shown that moral emotion has a stronger link to moral action than moral reasoning, citing Damasio's research on the [[somatic marker hypothesis]] and Batson's [[empathy-altruism]] hypothesis.<ref name="Haidt2001" />

Similarly, in his theory of moral satisficing, Gerd Gigerenzer argues that moral behavior is not solely a result of deliberate reasoning but also of social heuristics that are embedded in social environments. In other words, intuitionist theories can use heuristics to explain intuition. He emphasizes that these are key to understanding moral behavior.  Modifying moral behavior therefore entails changing heuristics and/or modifying environments rather than focussing on individuals.
