In this way, moral satisficing extends social intuitionism by adding both concrete heuristics and a focus on the environments with which the heuristics interact to produce behavior.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gigerenzer |first=Gerd |date=2008 |title=Moral intuition= fast and frugal heuristics? |journal=Moral Psychology |pages=1–26 |via=MIT Press}}</ref><ref>Gigerenzer, G. (2010). Moral satisficing: Rethinking moral behavior as bounded rationality. ''Topics in cognitive science'', ''2''(3), 528-554.</ref>

Following the publication of a landmark fMRI study in 2001,<ref name="Greene2001" /> [[Joshua Greene (psychologist)|Joshua Greene]] separately proposed his [[Dual process theory (moral psychology)|dual process theory of moral judgment]], according to which intuitive/emotional and deliberative processes respectively give rise to characteristically deontological and consequentialist moral judgments. A "[[Deontological ethics|deontologist]]" is someone who has rule-based morality that is mainly focused on duties and rights; in contrast, a "[[Consequentialism|consequentialist]]" is someone who believes that only the best overall consequences ultimately matter.<ref>{{cite book|title=Moral Psychology|last=Greene|first=Joshua|publisher=MIT Press|year=2008|isbn=978-0-262-69355-4|editor-last=Sinnott-Armstrong|editor-first=Walter|volume=3|location=Cambridge, Massachusetts|pages=35–80|chapter=The secret joke of Kant's Soul|oclc=750463100}}</ref>

===Moral emotions===
{{Main|Moral emotions}}
{{See also|Social emotions}}
Moral emotions are a variety of social emotion that are involved in forming and communicating moral judgments and decisions, and in motivating behavioral responses to one's own and others' moral behavior.<ref name="Pizarro2007b">{{cite book |last1=Pizarro |first1=David A. |editor1-last=Baumeister |editor1-first=Roy F |editor2-last=Vohs |editor2-first=Kathleen D |title=Encyclopedia of Social Psychology |url=https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasoci00baum |url-access=limited |date=2007 |publisher=SAGE Publications, Inc. |isbn=978-1-4129-5625-3 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/encyclopediasoci00baum/page/n629 588]–589 |chapter=Moral Emotions|doi=10.4135/9781412956253.n350 }}</ref><ref name="Haidt, J. 2003 p. 855">{{cite book|title=Handbook of Affective Sciences|last=Haidt|first=Jonathan|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2003|isbn=978-0-19-512601-3|editor-last=Davidson|editor-first=Richard|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofaffect0000unse/page/855 855]|chapter=The Moral Emotions|editor-last2=Scherer|editor-first2=Klaus|editor-last3=Goldsmith|editor-first3=H.|chapter-url=http://www.gruberpeplab.com/teaching/psych3131_spring2015/documents/4.1_Haidt2003_Moralemotions.pdf|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofaffect0000unse/page/855}}</ref><ref name="Tangney2007">{{cite journal |last1=Tangney |first1=June Price |last2=Stuewig |first2=Jeff |last3=Mashek |first3=Debra J. |title=Moral Emotions and Moral Behavior |journal=[[Annual Review of Psychology]] |date=January 2007 |volume=58 |issue=1 |pages=345–372 |doi=10.1146/annurev.psych.56.091103.070145 |pmid=16953797 |pmc=3083636 |url=http://www.its.caltech.edu/~squartz/Tangney.pdf}}</ref>
While moral reasoning has been the focus of most study of morality dating back to Plato and Aristotle, the emotive side of morality was historically looked upon with disdain in early moral psychology research.<ref name="Pizarro2007b" /> However, in the last 30–40 years, there has been a rise in a new front of research: moral emotions as the basis for moral behavior.<ref name="Tangney2007" /> This development began with a focus on [[empathy]] and [[Guilt (emotion)|guilt]], but has since moved on to encompass new scholarship on emotions such as [[anger]], [[shame]], [[disgust]], [[awe]], and [[Elevation (emotion)|elevation]]. While different moral transgressions have been linked to different emotional reactions, bodily reactions to such transgressions are not too different and can be characterized by some felt activations in the gut area as well as the head area.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Atari |first1=Mohammad |last2=Mostafazadeh Davani |first2=Aida |last3=Dehghani |first3=Morteza |date=February 2020 |title=Body Maps of Moral Concerns |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0956797619895284 |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=160–169 |doi=10.1177/0956797619895284 |pmid=31913779 |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

===Moralization and moral conviction===

Moralization, a term introduced to moral psychology by [[Paul Rozin]], refers to the process through which preferences are converted into values.<ref name="Rozin1999">{{cite journal |last1=Rozin |first1=Paul |title=The Process of Moralization |journal=Psychological Science |date=1999 |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=218–221 |doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00139|s2cid=145121850 }}</ref><ref name="Rozin1997">{{cite journal |last1=Rozin |first1=Paul |last2=Markwith |first2=Maureen |last3=Stoess |first3=Caryn |title=Moralization and Becoming a Vegetarian: The Transformation of Preferences Into Values and the Recruitment of Disgust |journal=Psychological Science |date=6 May 2016 |volume=8 |issue=2 |pages=67–73 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.1997.tb00685.x|s2cid=22267477 }}</ref><ref name="Rhee2019">{{cite journal |last1=Rhee |first1=Joshua J. |last2=Schein |first2=Chelsea |last3=Bastian |first3=Brock |title=The what, how, and why of moralization: A review of current definitions, methods, and evidence in moralization research |journal=Social and Personality Psychology Compass |date=25 November 2019 |volume=13 |issue=12 |article-number=e12511 |doi=10.1111/spc3.12511|s2cid=212770791 }}</ref> Relatedly, [[Linda Skitka]] and colleagues have introduced the concept of moral conviction, which refers to a "strong and absolute belief that something is right or wrong, moral or immoral."<ref>{{cite journal|last=Skitka|first=Linda|year=2002|title=Do the means always justify the ends or do the ends sometimes justify the means? A value protection model of justice|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/247746696|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|volume=28|issue=5|pages=452–461|doi=10.1177/0146167202288003|s2cid=145542300}}</ref><ref name="Skitka2021">{{cite journal |last1=Skitka |first1=Linda J. |last2=Hanson |first2=Brittany E. |last3=Scott Morgan |first3=G. |last4=Wisneski |first4=Daniel C. |title=The Psychology of Moral Conviction |journal=[[Annual Review of Psychology]] |date=4 January 2021 |volume=72 |issue=1 |pages=annurev–psych–063020-030612 |doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-063020-030612|pmid=32886586 |s2cid=221504252 }}</ref> According to Skitka's integrated theory of moral conviction (ITMC), attitudes held with moral conviction, known as moral mandates, differ from strong but non-moral attitudes in a number of important ways. Namely, moral mandates derive their motivational force from their perceived universality, perceived objectivity, and strong ties to emotion.<ref>{{cite book|title=Encyclopedia of Peace Psychology|last1=Morgan|first1=G. S.|last2=Skitka|first2=L. J.|publisher=Wiley-Blackwell|year=2011|isbn=978-1-4051-9644-4|editor=Christie|editor-first=Daniel J.|chapter=Moral conviction}}</ref> Perceived universality refers to the notion that individuals experience moral mandates as transcending persons and cultures; additionally, they are regarded as matters of fact. Regarding association with emotion, ITMC is consistent with Jonathan Haidt's social intuitionist model in stating that moral judgments are accompanied by discrete moral emotions (i.e., disgust, shame, guilt). Importantly, Skitka maintains that moral mandates are not the same thing as moral values. Whether an issue will be associated with moral conviction varies across persons.

One of the main lines of IMTC research addresses the behavioral implications of moral mandates. Individuals prefer greater social and physical distance from attitudinally dissimilar others when moral conviction was high. This effect of moral conviction could not be explained by traditional measures of attitude strength, extremity, or centrality. Skitka, Bauman, and Sargis placed participants in either attitudinally heterogeneous or homogenous groups to discuss procedures regarding two morally mandated issues, [[abortion]] and [[capital punishment]]. Those in attitudinally heterogeneous groups demonstrated the least amount of goodwill towards other group members, the least amount of cooperation, and the most tension/defensiveness. Furthermore, individuals discussing a morally mandated issue were less likely to reach a consensus compared to those discussing non-moral issues.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Skitka|first=L. J.|author2=Bauman, C.|author3=Sargis, E.|year=2005|title=Moral conviction: Another contributor to attitude strength or something more?|url=https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/7e7e/2fb7cf672e00b4ea00b4a75d4eee2fd51f5d.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180209002749/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/7e7e/2fb7cf672e00b4ea00b4a75d4eee2fd51f5d.pdf|archive-date=2018-02-09|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=88|issue=6|pages=895–917|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.88.6.895|pmid=15982112|s2cid=14291970}}</ref>

=== Moral Enhancement ===
''Main Article:'' [[Moral enhancement]]

Moral Enhancement (abbreviated ME), also called moral bioenhancement (abbreviated MBE), is the use of biomedical technology to morally improve individuals.<ref>{{Citation |title=Moral enhancement |date=2024-07-08 |work=Wikipedia |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Moral_enhancement&oldid=1233230636 |access-date=2024-11-10 |language=en}}</ref> There is another subdiscipline under moral enhancement which is denoted as Traditional Moral Enhancement (TME).  <ref name="Zarpentine 141–153">{{Cite journal |last=Zarpentine |first=Chris |date=April 2013 |title='The Thorny and Arduous Path of Moral Progress': Moral Psychology and Moral Enhancement |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s12152-012-9166-4 |journal=Neuroethics |language=en |volume=6 |issue=1 |pages=141–153 |doi=10.1007/s12152-012-9166-4 |issn=1874-5490|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Moral Enhancement is also considered the altering of moral behavior, moral traits, moral decision making, and or cognitive abilities but there hasn't been a clear cut definition of what it all entails. A scholar by the name of J.B.S. Haldane made a statement "it is only hopeful if mankind can adjust its morality to its powers." Haldane had a focus on using moral enhancement to improve the way society as whole functioned to increase the balance between morality and the usage of science.<ref name="Zarpentine 141–153"/>

==Intersections with other fields==
===Sociological applications===
Some research shows that people tend to self-segregate based on moral and political views,<ref name=Haidt2003>{{cite journal | last1 = Haidt | first1 = Jonathan | last2 = Rosenberg | first2 = Evan | last3 = Hom | first3 = Holly | year = 2003 | title = Differentiating Diversities: Moral Diversity Is Not Like Other Kinds | journal = Journal of Applied Social Psychology | volume = 33 | issue =1 | pages = 1–36 | doi=10.1111/j.1559-1816.2003.tb02071.x}}</ref><ref name=Motyl2014>{{cite journal | last1 = Motyl | first1 = Matt | last2 = Iyer | first2 = Ravi | last3 = Oishi | first3 = Shigehiro | last4 = Trawalterl | first4 = Sophie | last5 = Nosek | first5 = Brian A. | year = 2014 | title = How ideological migration geographically segregates groups | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 51 | pages = 1–14 | doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2013.10.010| url = http://psyarxiv.com/25myb/ }}</ref> exaggerate the magnitude of moral disagreements across political divides,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Graham |first1=Jesse |last2=Nosek |first2=Brian A. |last3=Haidt |first3=Jonathan |last4=Young |first4=Liane |title=The Moral Stereotypes of Liberals and Conservatives: Exaggeration of Differences across the Political Spectrum |journal=PLOS ONE |date=12 December 2012 |volume=7 |issue=12 |article-number=e50092 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0050092 |pmid=23251357 |pmc=3520939|bibcode=2012PLoSO...750092G |doi-access=free }}</ref> and avoid exposure to the opinions of those with opposing political views.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Frimer |first1=Jeremy A. |last2=Skitka |first2=Linda J. |last3=Motyl |first3=Matt |title=Liberals and conservatives are similarly motivated to avoid exposure to one another's opinions |journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology |date=September 2017 |volume=72 |pages=1–12 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2017.04.003|url=http://psyarxiv.com/mqzue/ }}</ref>

===Normative implications===
Researchers have begun to debate the implications (if any) moral psychology research has for other subfields of ethics such as [[normative ethics]] and [[meta-ethics]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kahane |first1=Guy |title=Evolutionary Debunking Arguments |journal=Noûs |date=March 2011 |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=103–125 |doi=10.1111/j.1468-0068.2010.00770.x|pmid=21949447 |pmc=3175808 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Greene |first1=Joshua |title=From neural 'is' to moral 'ought': what are the moral implications of neuroscientific moral psychology? |journal=Nature Reviews Neuroscience |date=October 2003 |volume=4 |issue=10 |pages=846–850 |doi=10.1038/nrn1224 |pmid=14523384|s2cid=14438498 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Greene |first1=Joshua |last2=Cohen |first2=Jonathan |title=For the law, neuroscience changes nothing and everything |journal= Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B: Biological Sciences|date=29 November 2004 |volume=359 |issue=1451 |pages=1775–1785 |doi=10.1098/rstb.2004.1546|pmid=15590618 |pmc=1693457 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Berker |first1=Selim |title=The Normative Insignificance of Neuroscience |journal=Philosophy & Public Affairs |date=September 2009 |volume=37 |issue=4 |pages=293–329 |doi=10.1111/j.1088-4963.2009.01164.x|s2cid=5952062 |url=http://nrs.harvard.edu/urn-3:HUL.InstRepos:4391332 }}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Nagel |first1=Thomas |title=You Can't Learn About Morality from Brain Scans |url=https://newrepublic.com/article/115279/joshua-greenes-moral-tribes-reviewed-thomas-nagel |magazine=The New Republic |date=2 November 2013}}</ref> For example [[Peter Singer]], citing Haidt's work on social intuitionism and Greene's dual process theory, presented an "[[evolutionary debunking]] argument" suggesting that the normative force of our moral intuitions is undermined by their being the "biological residue of our evolutionary history."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Singer |first1=Peter |title=Ethics and Intuitions |journal=The Journal of Ethics |date=October 2005 |volume=9 |issue=3–4 |pages=331–352 |doi=10.1007/s10892-005-3508-y|s2cid=49914215 }}</ref> [[John Michael Doris]] discusses the way in which [[Social psychology|social psychological]] experiments—such as the [[Stanford prison experiment]]s involving the idea of [[Situationism (psychology)|situationism]]—call into question a key component in [[virtue ethics]]: the idea that individuals have a single, environment-independent moral character.<ref>{{cite book|title=Lack of Character: Personality and Moral Behavior|last=Doris|first=John M.|date=2002|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-1-316-02549-9}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=February 2018}} As a further example, [[Shaun Nichols]] (2004) examines how empirical data on psychopathology suggests that [[moral rationalism]] is false.<ref>{{cite book|title=Sentimental Rules: On the Natural Foundations of Moral Judgment|last=Nichols|first=Shaun|date=2004|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-988347-9}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=February 2018}}

Additionally, research in moral psychology is being used to inform debates in applied ethics around [[moral enhancement]].<ref name="Darby2017">{{cite journal |last1=Darby |first1=R. Ryan |last2=Pascual-Leone |first2=Alvaro |title=Moral Enhancement Using Non-invasive Brain Stimulation |journal=Frontiers in Human Neuroscience |date=22 February 2017 |volume=11 |page=77 |doi=10.3389/fnhum.2017.00077|pmid=28275345 |pmc=5319982 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Levy2014">{{cite journal |last1=Levy |first1=Neil |last2=Douglas |first2=Thomas |last3=Kahane |first3=Guy |last4=Terbeck |first4=Sylvia |last5=Cowen |first5=Philip J. |last6=Hewstone |first6=Miles |last7=Savulescu |first7=Julian |title=Are You Morally Modified?: The Moral Effects of Widely Used Pharmaceuticals |journal=Philosophy, Psychiatry, & Psychology |date=2014 |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=111–125 |doi=10.1353/ppp.2014.0023|pmid=25892904 |pmc=4398979 }}</ref>

===Robotics and artificial intelligence===
{{Main|Machine ethics}}
{{See also|Robot ethics|Ethics of artificial intelligence|AI alignment}}

At the intersection of moral psychology and machine ethics, researchers have begun to study people's views regarding the potentially ethically significant decisions that will be made by [[self-driving car]]s.<ref name="Bonnefon2016" /><ref name="Awad2018" /><ref name="Awad2020">{{cite journal |last1=Awad |first1=Edmond |last2=Dsouza |first2=Sohan |last3=Bonnefon |first3=Jean-François |last4=Shariff |first4=Azim |last5=Rahwan |first5=Iyad |title=Crowdsourcing moral machines |journal=Communications of the ACM |date=24 February 2020 |volume=63 |issue=3 |pages=48–55 |doi=10.1145/3339904|doi-access=free |hdl=21.11116/0000-0007-4771-A |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=De Freitas |first1=Julian |last2=Anthony |first2=Sam E. |last3=Censi |first3=Andrea |last4=Alvarez |first4=George A. |title=Doubting Driverless Dilemmas |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |date=31 July 2020 |volume=15 |issue=5 |pages=1284–1288 |doi=10.1177/1745691620922201|pmid=32735472 |s2cid=220908883 |url=http://psyarxiv.com/a36e5/ }}</ref>

Mohammad Atari and his colleagues recently examined the moral psychology of the famous chatbot, [[ChatGPT]]. These authors asked in their title, "which humans?" — rhetorically pointing out that people should not ask how "human-like" machine morality is, but to which humans it resembles.<ref>{{Cite journal |title=OSF |url=https://osf.io/5b26t/ |access-date=2023-12-10 |website=osf.io |doi=10.31234/osf.io/5b26t |last1=Atari |first1=Mohammad |last2=Xue |first2=Mona J. |last3=Park |first3=Peter S. |last4=Blasi |first4=Damián Ezequiel |last5=Henrich |first5=Joseph }}</ref> These authors discovered that [[Large language model|Large Language Models]] (LLMs), especially ChatGPT, tend to echo moral values endorsed by Westerners, as their training datasets originate predominantly from Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD) societies.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Henrich |first=Joseph |title=The WEIRDest People in the World: How the West Became Psychologically Peculiar and Particularly Prosperous |date=2020-09-08 |publisher=Farrar, Straus and Giroux |isbn=978-0-374-17322-7 |location=New York |language=English}}</ref> This study points out that compared to the global average, people from WEIRD societies are more inclined toward individualism and impersonal prosocial behaviors while showing less traditionalism and group loyalty. The authors further highlighted that societies less aligned with these WEIRD moral values tend to experience greater misalignment with the moral values and outputs of [[ChatGPT]].<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Bloom |first=Paul |date=2023-11-29 |title=How Moral Can A.I. Really Be? |language=en-US |magazine=The New Yorker |url=https://www.newyorker.com/science/annals-of-artificial-intelligence/how-moral-can-ai-really-be |access-date=2023-12-10 |issn=0028-792X}}</ref>

Gerd Gigerenzer argued that the focus of AI ethics should reach far beyond the question whether an AI system has a moral bias or is able to exhibit human-like moral responses. It also needs to investigate the actual motives and ethical behavior of the people behind the AI. Contrary to the 1990s dream of an egalitarian internet providing honest and accurate information to all, various tech billionaires and politicians have highly succeeded in leveraging AI for their own purposes of surveillance and control, for tolerating systematic misinformation for profit, and for increasing their individual power to the detriment of a democracy.<ref>Gigerenzer, G. (2022). ''How to stay smart in a smart world: Why human intelligence still beats algorithms''. MIT Press.</ref>

Nebraska Symposium on Motivation |date=2005 |pages=119–163 |pmid=16335739 |url=https://www3.nd.edu/~dnarvaez/documents/NarvaezNebraska03.15.04.pdf |issn=0146-7875 |volume=51}}

* {{cite journal |last1=Narvaez |first1=D |year=2010 |title=Moral complexity: The fatal attraction of truthiness and the importance of mature moral functioning |url=https://www3.nd.edu/~dnarvaez/documents/MoralComplexity2010FINAL.pdf |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=163–181 |doi=10.1177/1745691610362351 |pmid=26162122 |citeseerx=10.1.1.187.2813 |s2cid=14465046}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Narvaez |first1=D |year=2012 |title=Moral neuroeducation from early life through the lifespan |url=http://library.allanschore.com/docs/NarvaezMoralNeuroeducation11.pdf |journal=Neuroethics |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=145–157 |doi=10.1007/s12152-011-9117-5 |s2cid=1617700 |access-date=2018-02-08 |archive-date=2017-09-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170918113755/http://library.allanschore.com/docs/NarvaezMoralNeuroeducation11.pdf }}
*{{cite journal |last1=Rozin |first1=P |year=1999 |title=The process of moralization |url=https://web.sas.upenn.edu/rozin/files/2016/09/MoralizationPS1999-1y8a9bo.pdf |journal=Psychological Science |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=218–221 |doi=10.1111/1467-9280.00139 |s2cid=145121850}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Rozin |first1=P. |last2=Lowery |first2=L. |last3=Imada |first3=S. |last4=Haidt |first4=J. |year=1999 |title=The CAD triad hypothesis: A mapping between three moral emotions (contempt, anger, disgust) and three moral codes (community, autonomy, divinity) |url=http://faculty.virginia.edu/haidtlab/articles/rozin.lowery.1999.moral-emotion-triad-hypothesis.pub012.pdf |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=76 |issue=4 |pages=574–586 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.76.4.574 |pmid=10234846 |access-date=2018-02-08 |archive-date=2017-03-01 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170301155848/http://faculty.virginia.edu/haidtlab/articles/rozin.lowery.1999.moral-emotion-triad-hypothesis.pub012.pdf }}
*{{cite journal |last1=Rozin |first1=P. |last2=Markwith |first2=M. |last3=Stoess |first3=C. |year=1997 |title=Moralization and becoming a vegetarian: The transformation of preferences into values and the recruitment of disgust |url=http://foodethics.univie.ac.at/fileadmin/user_upload/inst_ethik_wiss_dialog/Rozin__.._1997._Moralization_and_becoming_a_vegetarian_in__psychological_science.pdf |journal=Psychological Science |volume=8 |issue=2 |pages=67–73 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.1997.tb00685.x |s2cid=22267477}}
* {{cite book |last=Smith |first=Michael A. |title=The Moral Problem |location=Oxford |year=2004 |oclc=451103422 |publisher=Blackwell |isbn=978-0-631-18941-1}} 
* {{cite journal |last1=Tetlock |first1=P. |last2=Kristel |first2=O. |last3=Elson |first3=B. |last4=Green |first4=M. |last5=Lerner |first5=J. |year=2000 |title=The Psychology of the Unthinkable: Taboo Trade-Offs, Forbidden Base Rates, and Heretical Counterfactuals |url=https://www.academia.edu/download/44267432/The_Psychology_of_the_Unthinkable_Taboo_20160331-28670-1witok7.pdf |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |volume=78 |issue=5 |pages=853–870 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.78.5.853 |pmid=10821194 |bibcode=2000JPSP...78..853T |citeseerx=10.1.1.126.6275}}{{dead link|date=January 2025|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Thagard |first1=Paul |year=2007 |title=The Moral Psychology of Conflicts of Interest: Insights from Affective Neuroscience |url=http://watarts.uwaterloo.ca/~pthagard/Articles/moral-psych.2007.pdf |journal=Journal of Applied Philosophy |volume=24 |issue=4 |pages=367–380 |doi=10.1111/j.1468-5930.2007.00382.x}}
*{{cite book |last=Wallace |first=R. Jay |title=Normativity and the Will: Selected Essays on Moral Psychology and Practical Reason |year=2006 |publisher=Clarendon Press |isbn=978-0-19-153699-1}}
{{refend}}
